questions.prompt: Complete: "The doctor ___ treated me was very kind."
questions.explanation: Use who for people: the doctor who treated me. "which" is for things, not people; "where" is for places; and "whose" shows possession (the doctor whose car…), which is not the meaning here.

questions.prompt: Complete: "That's the village ___ my grandparents live."
questions.explanation: Use where for a place (= in which): the village where my grandparents live. "which"/"that" would need the place as an object (the village that I visited), and "who" is only for people.

questions.prompt: Complete: "I met a girl ___ brother plays for the national team."
questions.explanation: whose shows possession — it replaces her here (her brother): a girl whose brother plays. "who" would need a verb after it (a girl who plays), "which" is for things, and "who's" means "who is".

questions.prompt: Complete: "This is the bridge ___ connects the two parts of the city."
questions.explanation: A bridge is a thing, so use which (the doer of "connects"): the bridge which connects the two parts. "who" is only for people, "where" marks a place inside the clause, and "whose" shows possession.

options.text: The book what I bought is interesting.
The book who I bought is interesting.
The book where I bought is interesting.
The book that I bought is interesting.

questions.explanation: For a thing, use that (or which): the book that I bought. Never use "what" as a relative pronoun (a) — a very common error; "who" (b) is for people; and "where" (c) is for places, not a thing.
